drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.2 Epilepsy for Partial Onset Seizures (Adjunctive Therapy) The effectiveness of gabapentin as adjunctive therapy (added to other antiepileptic drugs) was established in multicenter placebo-controlled, double-blind, parallel-group clinical trials in adult and pediatric patients (3 years and older)

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

The responder rate at 600 mg (17%) was also not significantly higher than in the placebo, but the responder rate in the 1,800 mg group (26%) was statistically significantly superior to the placebo rate.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Monotherapy Epilepsy Patients with Partial-Onset or Primary Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizures Adults and Pediatric Patients 10 Years of Age and Older The effectiveness of topiramate as initial monotherapy in adults and pediatric patients 10 years of age and older with partial-onset or primary g

key_results: Forty-nine percent of patients had no prior AED treatment and 17% had a diagnosis of epilepsy for greater than 24 months.
In the double-blind phase, 470 patients were randomized to titrate up to 50 mg/day or 400 mg/day.
Similarity of exposure-response was also demonstrated in pediatric patients 6 to less than 16 years of age and adults when topiramate was given as initial monotherapy.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Epilepsy Monotherapy with Lamotrigine in Adults with Partial-Onset Seizures already Receiving Treatment with Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, or Primidone as the Single Antiepileptic Drug The effectiveness of monotherapy with lamotrigine was established in a multicenter, double-blind cl

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant (P<0.05).
efficacy_card.source_section: 14.1 Epilepsy Monotherapy with Lamotrigine in Adults with Partial-Onset Seizures already Receiving Treatment with Carbamazep

safety_card.boxed_warning: WARNING: SERIOUS SKIN RASHES Lamotrigine can cause serious rashes requiring hospitalization and discontinuation of treatment. The incidence of these rashes, which have included Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is approximately 0.3% to 0.8% in pediatric patients (aged 2 to 17 years) and 0.08% to 0.3% in adults receiving lamotrigine. One rash-related death was reported in a prospectively followed cohort of

key_results: 14.1 Partial-Onset Seizures Effectiveness in Partial-Onset Seizures in Adults The effectiveness of levetiracetam for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults was established in three multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical studies in patients who had refractory partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization.
In these studies, 904 patients were randomized to placebo, 1000 mg, 2000 mg, or 3000 mg/day.
The comparison of levetiracetam 2000 mg/day to levetiracetam 1000 mg/day for responder rate was statistically significant ( P = 0.02).

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

The responder rate at 600 mg (17%) was also not significantly higher than in the placebo, but the responder rate in the 1,800 mg group (26%) was statistically significantly superior to the placebo rate.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: Clinical Studies The effectiveness of zonisamide as adjunctive therapy (added to other antiepilepsy drugs) has been established in three multicenter, placebo-controlled, double blind, 3-month clinical trials (two domestic, one European) in 499 patients with refractory partial onset seizures with or

key_results: In the two U.S. studies, over 80% of patients were Caucasian; 100% of patients in the European study were Caucasian.

Both studies demonstrated statistically significant differences favoring zonisamide for doses of 400 mg/day to 600 mg/day, and there was no apparent difference between once daily and twice daily dosing (in different studies).
Analysis of the data (first 4 weeks) during titration demonstrated statistically significant differences favoring zonisamide at doses between 100 mg/day and 400 mg/day.

safety_card.common_adverse_reactions: ADVERSE REACTIONS The most common adverse reactions with zonisamide (an incidence at least 4% greater than placebo) in controlled clinical trials and shown in descending order of frequency were somnolence, anorexia, dizziness, ataxia, agitation/irritability, and difficulty with memory and/or concentration. drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Monotherapy Epilepsy Patients with Partial-Onset or Primary Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizures Adults and Pediatric Patients 10 Years of Age and Older The effectiveness of topiramate tablets as initial monotherapy in adults and pediatric patients 10 years of age and older with partial-onset or p

key_results: Forty-nine percent of patients had no prior AED treatment and 17% had a diagnosis of epilepsy for greater than 24 months.
In the double-blind phase, 470 patients were randomized to titrate up to 50 mg/day or 400 mg/day.
Similarity of exposure-response was also demonstrated in pediatric patients 6 to less than 16 years of age and adults when topiramate tablets was given as initial monotherapy.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Epilepsy Monotherapy with Lamotrigine in Adults with Partial-Onset Seizures Already Receiving Treatment with Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, or Primidone as the Single AED The effectiveness of monotherapy with lamotrigine was established in a multicenter double-blind clinical trial enr

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( p <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( p <0.05).

safety_card.boxed_warning: WARNING: SERIOUS SKIN RASHES Lamotrigine can cause serious rashes requiring hospitalization and discontinuation of treatment. The incidence of these rashes, which have included Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is approximately 0.3% to 0.8% in pediatric patients (aged 2 to 17 years) and 0.08% to 0.3% in adults receiving lamotrigine. key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.

Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design to compare a high dose (2,400 mg) with a low dose (300 mg) of oxcarbazepine, after substituting oxcarbazepine 2,400 mg/day for 1 or more AEDs.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p=0.0001.

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.05).

key_results: 14.1 Partial-Onset Seizures Effectiveness in Partial-Onset Seizures in Adults The effectiveness of levetiracetam for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults was established in three multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical studies in patients who had refractory partial onset seizures with or without secondary generalization.
In these studies, 904 patients were randomized to placebo, 1,000 mg, 2,000 mg, or 3,000 mg/day.
The comparison of Levetiracetam 2,000 mg/day to Levetiracetam 1,000 mg/day for responder rate was statistically significant (P=0.02).

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy. Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p = 0.0001.
The difference between the 2 treatments was statistically significant in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 2), p = 0.046.
The difference between the curves was statistically significant in favor of the oxcarbazepine 2400 mg/day group (see Figure 3), p = 0.0001.

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

key_results: 14.1 Levetiracetam extended-release tablets in Adults The effectiveness of levetiracetam extended-release tablets for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults was established in one multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical study across 7 countries in patients who had refractory partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization (Study 1).
The primary efficacy endpoint in Study 1 was the percent reduction over placebo in mean weekly frequency of partial-onset seizures.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.2 Epilepsy for Partial Onset Seizures (Adjunctive Therapy) The effectiveness of gabapentin as adjunctive therapy (added to other antiepileptic drugs) was established in multicenter placebo-controlled, double-blind, parallel-group clinical trials in adult and pediatric patients (3 years and older)

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

The responder rate at 600 mg (17%) was also not significantly higher than in the placebo, but the responder rate in the 1800 mg group (26%) was statistically significantly superior to the placebo rate.

key_results: The effectiveness of lamotrigine extended-release tablets as adjunctive therapy in partial-onset seizures, with or without secondary generalization, was established in a 19-week, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 236 patients aged 13 years and older (approximately 93% of patients were aged 16 to 65 years).
Approximately 36% were from the U.S. and approximately 64% were from other countries including Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Germany, India, Korea, Russian Federation, and Ukraine.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Epilepsy Monotherapy with Lamotrigine Tablets in Adults with Partial-Onset Seizures Already Receiving Treatment with Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, or Primidone as the Single Antiepileptic Drug The effectiveness of monotherapy with lamotrigine tablets was established in a multicenter,

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine tablets and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine tablets and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.05).

key_results: There was a 23% (P=.018) difference in percentage seizure frequency reduction in favor of felbamate.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: CLINICAL STUDIES The results of controlled clinical trials established the efficacy of felbamate as monotherapy and adjunctive therapy in adults with partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization and in partial and generalized seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome in chi

key_results: In the multicenter trial, the percentage of patients who met escape criteria was 40% (18/45) in the felbamate group and 78% (39/50) in the low-dose valproate group.
In the single-center trial, the percentage of patients who met escape criteria was 14% (3/21) in the felbamate group and 90% (19/21) in the low-dose valproate group.
There was a 23% (P=.018) difference in percentage seizure frequency reduction in favor of felbamate.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy. Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.

Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design to compare a high dose (2,400 mg) with a low dose (300 mg) of oxcarbazepine, after substituting oxcarbazepine 2,400 mg/day for 1 or more AEDs.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p=0.0001.

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.

key_results: The effectiveness of lamotrigine extended-release tablets as adjunctive therapy in partial-onset seizures, with or without secondary generalization, was established in a 19-week, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 236 patients aged 13 years and older (approximately 93% of patients were aged 16 to 65 years).
Approximately 36% were from the U.S. and approximately 64% were from other countries including Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Germany, India, Korea, Russian Federation, and Ukraine.

key_results: 14.1 Levetiracetam Extended-Release Tablets in Adults The effectiveness of levetiracetam extended-release tablets for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults was established in one multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical study across 7 countries in patients who had refractory partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization (Study 1).
The primary efficacy endpoint in Study 1 was the percent reduction over placebo in mean weekly frequency of partial-onset seizures.

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.05).

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

key_results: The effectiveness of lamotrigine extended-release as adjunctive therapy in partial-onset seizures, with or without secondary generalization, was established in a 19-week, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 236 patients aged 13 years and older (approximately 93% of patients were aged 16 to 65 years).

key_results: The effectiveness of lamotrigine extended-release as adjunctive therapy in partial-onset seizures, with or without secondary generalization, was established in a 19-week, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in 236 patients aged 13 years and older (approximately 93% of patients were aged 16 to 65 years).
Approximately 36% were from the U.S. and approximately 64% were from other countries including Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Germany, India, Korea, Russian Federation, and Ukraine.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: CLINICAL STUDIES The results of controlled clinical trials established the efficacy of felbamate as monotherapy and adjunctive therapy in adults with partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization and in partial and generalized seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome in chi

key_results: There was a 23% (P=.018) difference in percentage seizure frequency reduction in favor of felbamate.
Statistically significant differences in the effect on seizure frequency favored felbamate over placebo for total seizures (26% reduction vs.
5% increase, P < .001), atonic seizures (44% reduction vs.
7% reduction, P=.002), and generalized tonic-clonic seizures (40% reduction vs.

safety_card.boxed_warning: WARNING: 1. APLASTIC ANEMIA THE USE OF FELBAMATE IS ASSOCIATED WITH A MARKED INCREASE IN THE INCIDENCE OF APLASTIC ANEMIA. ACCORDINGLY, FELBAMATE SHOULD ONLY BE USED IN PATIENTS WHOSE EPILEPSY IS SO SEVERE THAT THE RISK OF APLASTIC ANEMIA IS DEEMED ACCEPTABLE IN LIGHT OF THE BENEFITS CONFERRED BY ITS USE (SEE INDICATIONS ). ORDINARILY, A PATIENT SHOULD NOT BE PLACED ON AND/OR CONTINUED ON FELBAMAT
safety_card.common_adverse_reactions: The most common adverse reactions seen in association with felbamate in adults during monotherapy are anorexia, vomiting, insomnia, nausea, and headache. The most common adverse reactions seen in association with felbamate in adults during adjunctive therapy are anorexia, vomiting, insomnia, nausea, dizziness, somnolence, and headache.

key_results: 14.1 Partial-Onset Seizures The efficacy of perampanel in partial-onset seizures, with or without secondary generalization, was studied in patients who were not adequately controlled with 1 to 3 concomitant AEDs in 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter trials (Studies 1, 2, and 3) in adult and pediatric patients (12 years of age and older).
The criterion for statistical significance was p<0.05.
A statistically significant decrease in seizure rate was observed at doses of 4 mg to 12 mg per day.

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p = 0.0001.
The difference between the 2 treatments was statistically significant in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 2), p = 0.046.
The difference between the curves was statistically significant in favor of the oxcarbazepine 2,400 mg/day group (see Figure 3), p = 0.0001.

key_results: 14.1 Partial-Onset Seizures The efficacy of perampanel in partial-onset seizures, with or without secondary generalization, was studied in patients who were not adequately controlled with 1 to 3 concomitant AEDs in 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter trials (Studies 1, 2, and 3) in adult and pediatric patients (12 years of age and older).
The criterion for statistical significance was p<0.05.
A statistically significant decrease in seizure rate was observed at doses of 4 to 12 mg per day.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Monotherapy for Partial-Onset Seizures The effectiveness of eslicarbazepine acetate as monotherapy for partial-onset seizures was established in two identical, dose-blinded historical control trials in a total of 365 patients with epilepsy (Study 1 and Study 2). In these trials, patients were r

key_results: In these trials, patients were randomized in a 2:1 ratio to receive either eslicarbazepine acetate 1,600 mg or 1,200 mg once daily, and their responses were compared to those of a historical control group.

Statistical superiority to the historical control was considered to be demonstrated if the upper limit from a 2-sided 95% confidence interval for the percentage of patients meeting exit criteria in patients receiving eslicarbazepine acetate remained below the lower 95% prediction interval of 65% derived from the historical control data.

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the GABARONE group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

The responder rate at 600 mg (17%) was also not significantly higher than in the placebo, but the responder rate in the 1800 mg group (26%) was statistically significantly superior to the placebo rate.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: CLINICAL STUDIES The results of controlled clinical trials established the efficacy of felbamate as monotherapy and adjunctive therapy in adults with partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization and in partial and generalized seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome in chi

key_results: There was a 23% (P=.018) difference in percentage seizure frequency reduction in favor of felbamate.
Statistically significant differences in the effect on seizure frequency favored felbamate over placebo for total seizures (26% reduction vs.
5% increase, P<.001), atonic seizures (44% reduction vs.
7% reduction, P=.002), and generalized tonic-clonic seizures (40% reduction vs.

safety_card.boxed_warning: BOXED WARNING 1. APLASTIC ANEMIA THE USE OF FELBAMATE IS ASSOCIATED WITH A MARKED INCREASE IN THE INCIDENCE OF APLASTIC ANEMIA. ACCORDINGLY, FELBAMATE SHOULD ONLY BE USED IN PATIENTS WHOSE EPILEPSY IS SO SEVERE THAT THE RISK OF APLASTIC ANEMIA IS DEEMED ACCEPTABLE IN LIGHT OF THE BENEFITS CONFERRED BY ITS USE (SEE INDICATIONS ). ORDINARILY, A PATIENT SHOULD NOT BE PLACED ON AND/OR CONTINUED ON FEL
safety_card.common_adverse_reactions: The most common adverse reactions seen in association with felbamate in adults during monotherapy are anorexia, vomiting, insomnia, nausea, and headache. The most common adverse reactions seen in association with felbamate in adults during adjunctive therapy are anorexia, vomiting, insomnia, nausea, dizziness, somnolence, and headache.

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.

Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design to compare a high dose (2,400 mg) with a low dose (300 mg) of oxcarbazepine, after substituting oxcarbazepine 2,400 mg/day for 1 or more AEDs.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p=0.0001.

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant (P<0.05).

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.2 Epilepsy for Partial Onset Seizures (Adjunctive Therapy) The effectiveness of gabapentin as adjunctive therapy (added to other antiepileptic drugs) was established in multicenter placebo-controlled, double-blind, parallel-group clinical trials in adult and pediatric patients (3 years and older)

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

The responder rate at 600 mg (17%) was also not significantly higher than in the placebo, but the responder rate in the 1800 mg group (26%) was statistically significantly superior to the placebo rate.

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Oral Suspension Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of Oxcarbazepine Oral Suspension as monotherapy.

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p = 0.0001.

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Extended-Release Tablets Primary Trial A multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, three-arm, parallel-group study (Study 1) in male and female adults with refractory partial-onset seizures (18 to 65 years of age, inclusive) was performed to examine the safety and efficacy of oxcarbazepine extended-release.
The criterion for statistical significance was p<0.05.
Subjects were randomized to one of three treatment groups and took oxcarbazepine extended-release (1200 or 2400 mg/day) or placebo.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy. Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p = 0.0001.
The difference between the 2 treatments was statistically significant in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 2), p = 0.046.
The difference between the curves was statistically significant in favor of the oxcarbazepine 2400 mg/day group (see Figure 3), p = 0.0001.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Epilepsy Monotherapy with Lamotrigine in Adults with Partial-Onset Seizures Already Receiving Treatment with Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, or Primidone as the Single Antiepileptic Drug The effectiveness of monotherapy with lamotrigine was established in a multicenter, double-blind cl

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant (P<0.05).

key_results: 14.1 Oxcarbazepine Monotherapy Trials RaFour randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of oxcarbazepine as monotherapy.

Two trials compared oxcarbazepine to placebo and 2 trials used a randomized withdrawal design to compare a high dose (2400 mg) with a low dose (300 mg) of oxcarbazepine, after substituting oxcarbazepine 2400 mg/day for 1 or more AEDs.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of oxcarbazepine (see Figure 1), p=0.0001.

key_results: 14.1 Partial-Onset Seizures Effectiveness in Partial-Onset Seizures in Adults The effectiveness of levetiracetam for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults was established in three multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical studies in patients who had refractory partial-onset seizures with or without secondary generalization.
In these studies, 904 patients were randomized to placebo, 1,000 mg, 2,000 mg, or 3,000 mg/day.
The comparison of levetiracetam 2,000 mg/day to levetiracetam 1,000 mg/day for responder rate was statistically significant (P=0.02).

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.
Responder rate was 23% (14/61) in the gabapentin group and 9% (6/66) in the placebo group; the difference between groups was statistically significant.

The responder rate at 600 mg (17%) was also not significantly higher than in the placebo, but the responder rate in the 1,800 mg group (26%) was statistically significantly superior to the placebo rate.

safety_card.boxed_warning: WARNING 1. APLASTIC ANEMIA THE USE OF FELBATOL ® (felbamate) IS ASSOCIATED WITH A MARKED INCREASE IN THE INCIDENCE OF APLASTIC ANEMIA. key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with LAMICTAL and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with LAMICTAL and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.05).
Drop attacks were significantly reduced by LAMICTAL (34%) compared with placebo (9%), as were tonic-clonic seizures (36% reduction versus 10% increase for LAMICTAL and placebo, respectively).

drugs.efficacy_snippet: Clinical Studies: The effectiveness of ZONEGRAN as adjunctive therapy (added to other antiepilepsy drugs) has been established in three multicenter, placebo-controlled, double blind, 3-month clinical trials (two domestic, one European) in 499 patients with refractory partial onset seizures with or w

key_results: In the two US studies, over 80% of patients were Caucasian; 100% of patients in the European study were Caucasian.

Both studies demonstrated statistically significant differences favoring ZONEGRAN for doses of 400-600 mg/day, and there was no apparent difference between once daily and twice daily dosing (in different studies).
Analysis of the data (first 4 weeks) during titration demonstrated statistically significant differences favoring ZONEGRAN at doses between 100 and 400 mg/day.

key_results: 14.1 TRILEPTAL Monotherapy Trials Four randomized, controlled, double-blind, multicenter trials, conducted in a predominately adult population, demonstrated the efficacy of TRILEPTAL as monotherapy.
There was a statistically significant difference in favor of TRILEPTAL (see Figure 1), p = 0.0001.
The difference between the 2 treatments was statistically significant in favor of TRILEPTAL (see Figure 2), p = 0.046.
The difference between the curves was statistically significant in favor of the TRILEPTAL 2400 mg/day group (see Figure 3), p = 0.0001.

key_results: A response ratio of -0.33 corresponds to a 50% reduction in seizure frequency.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Monotherapy for Partial-Onset Seizures The effectiveness of APTIOM as monotherapy for partial-onset seizures was established in two identical, dose-blinded historical control trials in a total of 365 patients with epilepsy (Study 1 and Study 2). In these trials, patients were randomized in a 2:

key_results: In these trials, patients were randomized in a 2:1 ratio to receive either APTIOM 1600 mg or 1200 mg once daily, and their responses were compared to those of a historical control group.

Statistical superiority to the historical control was considered to be demonstrated if the upper limit from a 2-sided 95% confidence interval for the percentage of patients meeting exit criteria in patients receiving APTIOM remained below the lower 95% prediction interval of 65% derived from the historical control data.
The primary endpoint was the cumulative 112-day exit rate in the efficacy population.

key_results: 14 CLINICAL STUDIES The effectiveness of NAYZILAM for the acute treatment of intermittent, stereotypic episodes of frequent seizure activity (i.e., seizure clusters, acute repetitive seizures) that are distinct from a patient's usual seizure pattern in patients with epilepsy 12 years of age and older was established in a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (Study 1; NCT 01390220).
Study 1 was conducted in two phases: an open-label Test Dose Phase followed by a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, Comparative Phase.

Patients enrolled in the studies had partial-onset seizures with or without seconda

key_results: 14 CLINICAL STUDIES The efficacy of XCOPRI for the treatment of partial-onset seizures was established in two multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled studies in adult patients (Study 1 and Study 2).
More than 80% of patients were taking 2 or more concomitant AEDs.
Table 6 summarizes the results of the primary endpoint for XCOPRI in each study.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14 CLINICAL STUDIES The efficacy of ZONISADE is based upon a bioavailability study comparing ZONISADE oral suspension to zonisamide capsules in healthy subjects. The clinical studies information described below pertains to the zonisamide capsule formulation. The effectiveness of zonisamide as adjunc

key_results: In the two US studies, over 80% of patients were Caucasian; 100% of patients in the European study were Caucasian.

Both studies demonstrated statistically significant differences favoring zonisamide for doses of 400-600 mg/day, and there was no apparent difference between once daily and twice daily dosing (in different studies).
Analysis of the data (first 4 weeks) during titration demonstrated statistically significant differences favoring zonisamide at doses between 100 and 400 mg/day.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Monotherapy Epilepsy Patients with Partial-Onset or Primary Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizures Adults and Pediatric Patients 10 Years of Age and Older The effectiveness of topiramate as initial monotherapy in adults and pediatric patients 10 years of age and older with partial-onset or primary g

key_results: Forty-nine percent of patients had no prior AED treatment and 17% had a diagnosis of epilepsy for greater than 24 months.
In the double-blind phase, 470 patients were randomized to titrate up to 50 mg/day or 400 mg/day.
Similarity of exposure response was also demonstrated in pediatric patients 6 to less than 16 years of age and adults when topiramate was given as initial monotherapy.

key_results: 14.1 Monotherapy in Patients with Partial-Onset Seizures The efficacy of immediate-release lacosamide in monotherapy was established in a historical-control, multicenter, randomized trial that included 425 patients, age 16 to 70 years, with partial-onset seizures (Study 1).
Patients were randomized 3 to 1 to receive lacosamide 400 mg/day or lacosamide 300 mg/day.

drugs.efficacy_snippet: 14.1 Epilepsy Monotherapy with Lamotrigine in Adults with Partial-Onset Seizures Already Receiving Treatment with Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, or Primidone as the Single Antiepileptic Drug The effectiveness of monotherapy with lamotrigine was established in a multicenter, double-blind cl

key_results: For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of all partial-onset seizures was 36% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 7% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.01).

For the intent-to-treat population, the median reduction of major motor seizures was 32% in patients treated with lamotrigine and 9% on placebo, a difference that was statistically significant ( P <0.05).

safety_card.common_adverse_reactions: 6 ADVERSE REACTIONS Epilepsy: Most common adverse reactions (incidence ≥10%) in adults were dizziness, headache, diplopia, ataxia, nausea, blurred vision, somnolence, rhinitis, pharyngitis, and rash. Additional adverse reactions (incidence ≥10%) reported in children included vomiting, infection, fever, accidental injury, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and tremor.
